g.data-narration: Welcome to Image Processing Basics - the foundation of computer vision!
Image processing uses mathematical operations to transform images - enhancing quality, extracting information, or preparing for analysis.
g.text: Manipulating digital images using mathematical operations to:

g.data-narration: There are three main types: Point operations work on individual pixels, Neighborhood operations use convolution, and Global operations use the entire image.
Point operations modify each pixel independently without considering neighbors. Key operations include brightness adjustment and contrast stretching.
g.text: Operate on individual pixels independently - no neighbor information needed.

g.data-narration: Let's visualize: adding constant brightens, multiplying increases contrast, inverting creates negatives, and thresholding creates binary images.
g.text: Brightness: Add constant c to each pixel value
Contrast: Multiply by factor a (a greater 1 increases, a less 1 decreases)

g.data-narration: Convolution applies a small matrix kernel across the entire image - computing weighted sum of neighboring pixels for each output pixel.
g.text: A kernel (small matrix, usually 3x3) slides across the image:

g.data-narration: Different kernels produce different effects: Identity keeps image unchanged, Box Blur averages neighbors, Gaussian blurs naturally, and Sharpen enhances edges.
Smoothing filters reduce noise by averaging pixels. Box filter is fast, Gaussian is natural, median removes salt-and-pepper, bilateral preserves edges.
g.text: Reduce noise by averaging/filtering neighboring pixels:

g.data-narration: Sharpening filters enhance edges and fine details using high-pass kernels that emphasize differences between pixels and their neighbors.
g.text: Enhance edges and fine details by emphasizing differences:

g.data-narration: Morphological operations work on binary images using a structuring element. Erosion shrinks white regions; dilation expands them.
g.text: Binary image operations using structuring element (typically 3x3):

g.data-narration: Opening is erosion followed by dilation - removes noise while preserving shape. Closing is dilation followed by erosion - fills holes while preserving shape.
A histogram shows intensity frequency. Equalization redistributes intensities using CDF to use the full 0-255 range, improving contrast.
Geometric transformations change image geometry: translation shifts position, rotation turns, scaling resizes, and affine combines multiple operations.
g.text: Change position, size, or orientation of pixels:

g.data-narration: Image arithmetic combines images: addition blends, subtraction detects changes, weighted blending combines with alpha, and bitwise operations handle masking.
Key takeaways: Point operations modify individual pixels, convolution applies kernels for filtering, morphological ops work on binary images, and histogram equalization improves contrast.
